5 LLM-Driven Program Synthesis
This section presents the LLM-driven program synthesis workflow that powersAUTOPOIESIS’s control plane.
5.1 Problem Formulation

Objective.Given a runtime trace that captures evolving workload and cluster conditions (i.e., the snapshotted runtime trace mentioned in §4),AUTOPOIESISseeks to evolve a serving policy that minimizes the end-to-end trace completion time Ttotal.

Continuousexecutionconstraint.AsisstandardinonlineLLMserving[2,11,12],thesystemoperates continuously:It does not pause during scheduling or reconfiguration.When a rescheduling event is triggered, the scheduler computes a new serving plan while the cluster continues serving under the previous (now st

Serving policy formulation.Under the continuous execution constraint, navigating the runtime trade-off space reduces to two decisions:When to reschedule and how to reschedule.We formalize these as a pair of co-evolved functions that together constitute the serving policy:

•should_reschedule(ctx) →{0, 1}, the rescheduling strategy that determines whether current condi- tions have shifted sufficiently to justify rescheduling.This controls the rescheduling frequency N within the given trace (Trade-off (i)).

•schedule(ctx) →plan, the scheduling algorithm that produces a new serving plan, which controls both the scheduling overhead (Trade-off (ii)) and the reconfiguration magnitude of each new plan relative to the old plan.(Trade-off (iii)).

5.2 Workflow Overview

The program synthesis workflow addresses the challenge of navigating this trade-off space by combining the code-generation capabilities of an LLM with population-based exploration to systematically explore the serving policy space.It proceeds as follows:

•Step 1:Initialization.A population of candidate policies is initialized according to the synthesis configura- tion (§5.4).Each candidate consists of a schedule and should_reschedule function pair, representing a complete serving policy.
•Step 2:Mutation.In each iteration, the LLM mutates candidates from the current population to generate new policies, guided by the synthesis configuration (§5.4).

•Step 3:Evaluation and feedback.The evaluator (§5.3) executes the should_reschedule and schedule functions of each candidate on the snapshotted runtime trace, producing both the fitness score Ttotaland structured artifact feedback to guide subsequent mutations (§5.4).

•Step 4:Selection and iteration.The synthesis workflow employs a MAP-Elites-inspired [23, 33] candidate selection mechanism combined with island-based population management [36] to retain high-performing candidateswhilemaintainingpopulationdiversity.Steps2-4repeatuntiltheevolutionaryprocess converge
The illustration of this workflow is shown in Figure 5.This workflow operates within the control plane (§4), running asynchronously alongside live serving in the data plane.
